text:
Admission Date:  2015-10-22       Discharge Date:  2003-12-16

Date of Birth:   2005-06-08       Sex:  M

Service:  Fairfield Memorial Hospital MEDICINE

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS (COVERS TIME ON Fairfield Memorial Hospital FIRM
BEGINNING WITH AN INTERN ACCEPT NOTE CONTAINING THE MICU
COURSE.):  This is a 45-year-old gentleman, with past medical
history significant for a CVA secondary to Riverview Hospital
abuse, type 2 diabetes mellitus, and chronic severe
hypertension, admitted on 2005-07-23 with severe GI bleed.
On arrival in the Emergency Department, the patient was found
to be nonresponsive with a systolic blood pressure of 100,
and a hematocrit of 11.3.  The patient was also found to have
acute renal failure with a creatinine of 4.3.  No baseline
creatinine was available.  The patient was admitted to the
Medical Intensive Care Unit for further care.  On the evening
of admission, he began to have bilateral lower extremity
shaking with rare upper extremity movement.  A neuro
consultation was obtained, and the patient was loaded with
Dilantin, although a seizure disorder, or seizure activity
were felt to be very low on the differential.

In the early morning on the day following admission, the
patient required intubation to control multiple aspects of
his medical situation including extreme hypertension,
respiratory distress, and the need for sedation secondary to
ongoing violent shaking of his extremities.  Later in the
day, on 1992-09-01, an EGD was performed which showed red
blood in the stomach and second part of the duodenum.  An
ulcer in the duodenum was treated with epinephrine injection
and thermal therapy.  The patient was given multiple
transfusions in the Medical Intensive Care Unit with an
appropriate increase in his hematocrit.  He was stable over
the next week.

On admission, the patient was found to have cardiac enzymes
which were positive for myocardial infarction.  A head CT was
obtained on 06-07 which showed no acute changes from previous
studies.  EEG performed on 01-09 showed slowing consistent
with encephalopathy.  LP was negative.  Biopsy obtained
during the EGD returned on 01-16 positive for H. pylori, and
the patient was started on treatment.

On 09-10, the patient was successfully extubated, but then
later developed copious secretions with respiratory distress
requiring reintubation.  On that day, he also began to have
fever spikes in the 101s.  His white blood cell began to
trend up, and blood cultures were obtained.  Sputum culture
from 2007-12-15 grew gram-positive cocci in pairs.
Therefore, the patient began empiric treatment with
vancomycin.  There was also a concern for sinusitis, and the
patient was treated with Augmentin.

A trach was performed on 09-10.  On 1978-10-17, he was in
stable condition and was transferred to the floor for further
care.

EXAM ON ADMISSION:  Blood pressure 135/55, heart rate 66,
respiratory rate 16, oxygen saturation 97% on room air,
temperature 98.2.
GENERAL:  Alert.  Able to answer yes and no questions.
HEENT:  Normocephalic, atraumatic.  Pupils equal, round and
reactive to light.  Unable to perform extraocular movements,
although this does appear secondary to understanding.  Moist
mucous membranes.
CARDIAC:  Regular rate and rhythm.
PULMONARY:  Clear to auscultation anteriorly and laterally.
ABDOMEN:  Soft.  Nontender, nondistended.  Positive bowel
sounds.
EXTREMITIES:  No clubbing, cyanosis or edema.  2+ dorsalis
pedis pulses bilaterally.
NEURO:  Cranial nerves II through XII intact, except
extraocular motion as described above.  5/5 strength in the
upper and lower extremities bilaterally.

LABS ON ADMISSION (ORIGINAL LABS FROM 2005-07-23):  White
blood cells at 8.3, hemoglobin 3.4, hematocrit 11.3,
neutrophils 76.1, bands 0, lymphocytes 20.6, monocytes 2.9,
eosinophils 0.2, basophils 0.1, platelets 232, PT 14.3, PTT
21.6, INR 1.4, sodium 138, potassium 4.2, chloride 105,
bicarb 22, BUN 81, creatinine 4.3, glucose 145, ALT 16, AST
26, CK 194, alkaline phosphatase 45, amylase 99, total
bilirubin 0.2, albumin 3.2, calcium 8.1, phosphorus 3.5,
magnesium 2.0.

LABS ON 1978-10-17 (AT WHICH TIME THE PATIENT WAS TRANSFERRED
TO THE Fairfield Memorial Hospital SERVICE):  White blood cell count 13.0,
hematocrit 37.0, platelets 436.  Differential - 70.3
neutrophils, 23.1 lymphocytes, 5.1 monocytes.  Chemistries -
sodium 146, potassium 3.6, chloride 109, bicarb 28, BUN 29,
creatinine 2.6, glucose 76, calcium 8.8, phosphorus 3.9,
magnesium 2.5.
CHEST X-RAY (AT THAT TIME):  Showed unchanged linear
bilateral bibasilar atelectasis without focal consolidation.

BRIEF SUMMARY OF HOSPITAL COURSE ON Fairfield Memorial Hospital FIRM - 1) FEVER:
The patient had first began spiking fevers on 1970-09-15.  He
continued to be intermittently febrile over the next week.
Sinusitis was considered to be the most likely source, given
copious yellow/green respiratory secretions.  For the
probable sinus infection, the patient was treated with a
14-day course of Augmentin.  In addition, the patient was
found to be growing gram-positive cocci in his sputum and was
started on vancomycin empirically in the Medical Intensive
Care Unit.  The vancomycin was dosed per level.  On
1994-11-05, the vancomycin was discontinued, as the patient's
white blood cell count was trending down, and his fever curve
was resolving.  Over the next few days, the patient became
afebrile, and has been afebrile since that time.  Multiple
blood cultures were obtained during the fever spikes, and
these have all been no growth final read.  In addition, the
patient had multiple negative C. difficile cultures.

2) HYPERTENSION:  The patient has a long history of chronic,
severe, uncontrolled hypertension.  Medications were adjusted
for better hypertensive control.  At this time, the patient
is on a clonidine patch, metoprolol 150 tid, nifedipine 60 q
6 h, hydralazine 75 q 6 h, and lisinopril 5 mg po qd.  On
this regimen, the patient's blood pressure has been
well-controlled.  For example, on 1991-05-17, it ranged
between 100-130/72-80.  This regimen will be continued at
discharge.

3) GI BLEED:  The patient was found to have a bleeding
duodenal ulcer, for which he was treated during the EGD.  His
hematocrit has been stable and within range since that time
with no active bleeding.  Crits were followed as needed.  In
addition, the patient's biopsy was positive for H. pylori,
and he received a 2-week course of treatment, including
clarithromycin and a PPI.

4) PULMONARY:  The patient did well with a tracheostomy on
the floor.  He consistently had saturations in the high-90s
to 100% range on 30-40% humidified trach mask.  Over the
course of the next 2 weeks, the patient was transitioned to
room air and continued to sat in the high-90s.  At this time,
pulmonary is being consulted, and there is consideration to
remove the patient's tracheostomy.

5) ANOXIC BRAIN INJURY:  It was thought that the patient had
an anoxic brain injury during his episode of severe anemia
and hypertension in the Emergency Department on presentation.
Over the course of 3 weeks on the floor, the patient has
made significant improvement while working with speech
therapy.  He is able to carry on a simple conversation, and
answer simple questions.  No further imaging or work-up was
indicated.

6) RENAL FAILURE:  In treating the patient and following his
creatinine, it appears that he has some baseline renal
dysfunction secondary to his severe chronic hypertension and
diabetes.  His baseline creatinine appears to be between 2.3
and 2.6.  It has been fairly stable inside this range.  As an
ACE inhibitor was added to control his blood pressure, his
creatinine was followed closely, but did not have a
significant long-term increase.

7) NEURO:  Neuro continued to follow the patient on the
floor.  After discussing the jerking movements of his
extremities with them, reviewing imaging data, and reviewing
findings of the EEG, it appeared very unlikely that the
patient had had a seizure, or had any sort of underlying
seizure disorder.  Therefore, he was slowly tapered off of
Dilantin and has been doing well since that time.  He will
need no further follow-up for this issue.

8) TYPE 2 DIABETES MELLITUS:  The patient had good blood
sugar control throughout his admission to the Lakes Regional Healthcare Firm.
He was covered with regular insulin sliding scale.

9) FEN:  Initially, the patient was on tube feeds through a
Dobbhoff tube.  However, he underwent a speech and swallow
study and was cleared to take thickened liquids and purees
without risk of aspiration.  He tolerated this diet well.  On
1991-05-17, he underwent another speech and swallow
evaluation to see if his diet could be advanced; these
results are currently pending.

10) REHAB:  PT, OT and speech therapy worked with the patient
throughout his time at Montefiore New Rochelle Hospital.

11) PROPHYLAXIS:  Subcu heparin; PPI; bowel regimen.

The remainder of this dictation will be finished at a later
time.




                            [**First Name11 (Name Pattern1) 2515**] Perez, M.D. [**MD Number(1) **]

Dictated By:John Hansen
MEDQUIST36

T:  1991-05-17  14:02
